examples.title: Peterson's Algorithm in C
examples.description: Implementation of Peterson's solution for two processes using shared variables
examples.explanation: Peterson's solution uses two flags indicating desire to enter and a turn variable. Both processes declare their interest, give priority to the other, and wait only if the other wants to enter and it's the other's turn.

examples.title: Test-and-Set Lock Implementation
examples.description: Using atomic test-and-set instruction to implement mutual exclusion lock
examples.code:
import threading

class TestAndSetLock:
    def __init__(self):
        self.lock = threading.Lock()  # Python's lock for demonstration
        self._locked = False          # Shared lock variable
    
    def test_and_set(self):
        """Atomic test-and-set operation"""
        # In actual hardware, this would be a single atomic instruction
        # Here we simulate using Python's atomic swap
        import ctypes
        old_value = self._locked
        self._locked = True
        return old_value
    
    def acquire(self):
        """Entry section - busy wait until lock is acquired"""
        while self.test_and_set():
            pass  # Busy wait - spinlock
    
    def release(self):
        """Exit section - release the lock"""
        self._locked = False

# Usage
lock = TestAndSetLock()

def process_code():
    lock.acquire()      # Entry section
    # Critical section - access shared resources
    print("In critical section")
    lock.release()      # Exit section
examples.explanation: Test-and-Set atomically reads the lock value and sets it to true in one indivisible operation. The acquire function busy-waits until the lock was previously free (returned false), then proceeds into the critical section.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case - depends on contention

examples.title: Simple Lock using AtomicInteger
examples.description: Implementing mutual exclusion using Java's atomic operations
examples.explanation: Java's AtomicBoolean provides compareAndSet which is atomic (equivalent to Test-and-Set). The lock is acquired by atomically setting from false to true. The critical section increments a shared counter. The exit section releases the lock by setting it back to false.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case depending on thread contention

examples.title: Bakery Algorithm Pseudocode
examples.description: Lamport's bakery algorithm for N processes achieving deadlock-free and starvation-free mutual exclusion
examples.explanation: The bakery algorithm assigns unique increasing ticket numbers. A process waits until no one is choosing a ticket, and then waits until its ticket is the smallest (breaking ties by process ID). This guarantees fairness (bounded waiting), progress, and mutual exclusion.
examples.timeComplexity: O(N) for entry section, N is number of processes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(N) - arrays of size N for choosing and number variables
